coredata.dc:title: Application of coated green source carbon dots with silica molecularly imprinted polymers as a fluorescence probe for selective and sensitive determination of phenobarbital
coredata.dc:creator: Shariati, Rana
Rezaei, Behzad
Jamei, Hamid Reza
Ensafi, Ali Asghar
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a selective and sensitive fluorescence sensor was developed for the detection of phenobarbital, an epilepsy drug, using molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) coated on the surface of green source carbon dots (GSCDs). First, GSCDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal method using Cedrus as a carbon source. Then, a MIPs-GSCDs as a fluorescence probe was obtained by coating a thin film of silica on the surface of the GSCDs using a reverse micro emulsion method. In this step, phenobarbital, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) were applied as a template, a functional monomer, and cross linker, respectively. The fluorescence signal of MIPs-GSCDs was selectively quenched by phenobarbital rebinding with MIP cavities. The fluorescence quenching signal was applied for phenobarbital sensing at the pH = 8 without the interference of other materials. After optimizing the factors affecting the sensor's response, a linear range between 0.4 and 34.5 nmol L−1 with a detection limit of 0.1 nmol L−1 was obtained. The sensor's capability in the real sample analysis was investigated by phenobarbital determination in a human blood plasma samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence sensor
Green source carbon dot
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Cedrus
Phenobarbital

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of MIPs-GSCDs and NIPs-GSCDs composite
Characterization of GSCDs, MIPs-GSCDs, and NIPs-GSCDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the support of Isfahan University of Technology (IUT)’s Research Council and Center of Excellence in Sensor and Green Chemistry .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of (A): GSCDs, (B): MIPs-GSCDs, (C): NIPs-GSCDs.

(A): DLS analysis of the GSCDs, (B): XRD pattern of the GSCDs (C): (a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum (b) fluorescence emission spectrum of the GSCDs, (D): Fluorescence emission spectra of the GSCDs at different excitation wavelength, (E): FT-IR spectra of (a) GSCDs, (b) NIPs-GSCDs, (c) MIPs-GSCDs after elution, (d) MIPs-GSCDs before elution, (e) Phenobarbital.
The structures of (A): phenobarbital, (B): TEOS and (C): APTES.
(A): UV–Vis absorption spectra of extraction mixtures after each wash, (B): (a) UV–Vis spectra of phenobarbital and (b) fluorescence spectra of MIPs-GSCDs.

(A): Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of MIPs-GSCDs at different pH (to 4.0 nmol L−1 phenobarbital), (B): Effect of different amount of the MIPs-GSCDs composite (to 4.0 nmol L−1 phenobarbital on the response at pH = 8.0), (C): Effect of temperature (in the range of 25–50 °C) on the fluorescence intensity of MIPs-GSCDs (500 mg L−1 of MIPs-GSCDs, 4.0 nmol L−1 of phenobarbital at pH = 8.0), (D): Effect of time (in the range 0–20 min) on the fluorescence intensity of MIPs-GSCDs (500 mg L−1 of MIPs-GSCDs, 4.0 nmol L−1 of phenobarbital at pH = 8.0).

(A): Fluorescence emission spectra of MIPs-GSCDs, (B): Fluorescence emission spectra of NIPs-GSCDs with the addition of different concentrations of phenobarbital (0–34.5 nmol L−1), (C): The Stern–Volmer curve, (D): Calibration curve.

Comparison of fluorescence intensity of MIPs-GSCDs, NIPs-GSCDs, and GSCDs after the addition of different species (300.0 nmol L−1) in the presence of 4.0 nmol L−1 phenobarbital at the optimum conditions.
Comparison of performance with different methods.
Results of the determination of phenobarbital in human blood plasma samples (n = 4).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average values for three determinations ± s.

head.ce:title: Application of coated green source carbon dots with silica molecularly imprinted polymers as a fluorescence probe for selective and sensitive determination of phenobarbital

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156-83111, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a selective and sensitive fluorescence sensor was developed for the detection of phenobarbital, an epilepsy drug, using molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) coated on the surface of green source carbon dots (GSCDs). First, GSCDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal method using Cedrus as a carbon source. Then, a MIPs-GSCDs as a fluorescence probe was obtained by coating a thin film of silica on the surface of the GSCDs using a reverse micro emulsion method. In this step, phenobarbital, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) were applied as a template, a functional monomer, and cross linker, respectively. The fluorescence signal of MIPs-GSCDs was selectively quenched by phenobarbital rebinding with MIP cavities. The fluorescence quenching signal was applied for phenobarbital sensing at the pH = 8 without the interference of other materials. After optimizing the factors affecting the sensor's response, a linear range between 0.4 and 34.5 nmol L−1 with a detection limit of 0.1 nmol L−1 was obtained. The sensor's capability in the real sample analysis was investigated by phenobarbital determination in a human blood plasma samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Epilepsy is one of the most common brain disorders and phenobarbital is broadly used for the treatment of certain types of this neurological disorder [1]. Furthermore, Phenobarbital overdosage may cause sedation and hypnosis [2] and may also lead to symptoms such as shallow breathing, drowsiness, decreased urination and level of consciousness. The clinical symptoms of epilepsy are not easily controlled, thus clinicians have tried to determine the toxicity and health effects of these antiepileptic drugs [3]. Capillary electrophoresis [4], voltammetry method [5], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6–9], gas-liquid chromatography [10] and liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry [11] are some of the methods used for the determination of phenobarbital in pharmaceutical formulations or biological media. Because of the complexity of biological samples, most of the reported techniques require some pretreatment and sample cleaning steps.

Molecular imprinting is a technique used for creating selective recognition sites in polymeric materials in which a target molecule and functional monomers are co-polymerized [12]. Then, the target molecules are removed from a polymer, creating a cavity which works as a selective binding site for the specific target molecule [13]. The main advantages of MIPs are high selectivity, high physical validity, thermal stability in acidic and basic conditions, low-cost and easy preparation [14]. Furthermore, MIPs can be used for sample clean-up and selective extraction of analyte in complex matrixes. Incomplete template removal, low binding capacity, and slow mass transfer are some of the drawbacks of traditional MIPs. Surface imprinting can be used to eliminate those impediments. Imprinted targets in this method are placed on the surface or near the surface of the material. Surface imprinted polymers have advantages such as faster mass transfer, binding kinetics and higher binding capacity [15].

Luminescence methods are simple and very sensitive approaches which have been used for analyzing different pharmaceutical and medical species. Carbon dots (CDs) are brightly luminescent nanoparticles with many unique properties including bright fluorescence, easy synthesis, low cytotoxicity, good stability and convenient surface functionalization [16,17]. Carbon dots can be used as a support for surface imprinting process to prepare the selective and sensitive probe.

Therefore, in the present work, a new selective and sensitive phenobarbital fluorescence sensor is introduced based on MIPs-GSCDs. Green source carbon dots were synthesized from Cedrus using the hydrothermal method while the polymers were prepared using reverse micro emulsion method. To prepare embedded MIPs with GSCDs, phenobarbital was used as the target molecule, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) as a functional monomer and tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) as a cross linker. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) was used for the characterization of the synthesized GSCDs.

Triton X-100, TEOS, APTES, cyclohexane, ammonium hydroxide solution (25–28%), Phenobarbital, acetone, acetonitrile, trichloroacetic acid ethanol, and ethanol were all purchased from Aldrich (London, UK). Acetic acid (CH3COOH), boric acid (H3BO3) and phosphoric acid (H3PO4) were used for preparing Universal buffer solution (pH = 8.0) and the pH was regulated with sodium hydroxide (NaOH) 0.2 mol L−1 solution.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded by a Jasco FP–750 spectrofluorometers. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were performed on KBr plate with a Jasco 680-plus spectrophotometer. UV–Vis spectra were measured with a double beam Jasco V–570 UV–Vis/NIR spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy imaging was performed with Philips CM30 300 kV TEM operating at 300 kV. Dynamic light scattering was used for obtaining the GSCDs size distribution. Zeta potential measurement was carried out with Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS, Model ZEN3600. Amicon Ultra-4centrifugal filter unit with 10 kDa MW cut-off was used to purify the GSCDs.

Green source carbon dots were synthesized from Cedrus using the hydrothermal method. Briefly¸ the Cedrus was completely pulverized, and 1.0 g of the resulting powder was added to 80 mL of distilled water. The mixture was stirred for 6 min and heated to 180 ͦ C for 12 h. Then, the solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 30 min. Afterward, the product was filtered through a 0.45-µm Millipore filter to eliminate any probable particulation and kept in a dark place at room temperature. Green source carbon dots were synthesized with a quantum yield of 18.6% through the hydrothermal method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MIPs-GSCDs and NIPs-GSCDs composite
ce:section.ce:para: The reverse micro emulsion is one of the possible methods used to prepare silica-coated CDs. Typically; 7.5 mL of cyclohexane was added to 1.8 mL of Triton X-100 and stirred for 15 min. Next, 100 µL ammonium hydroxide (25–28% v/v), 400 µL GSCDs and 50 µL TEOS were added to the above solution and stirred for 2 h. Then, 5 mg of phenobarbital and 20 µL of APTES were added to the above solution. Next, the solution was stirred for 12 h at room temperature. Afterward, the micro emulsion was broken by 20 mL of acetone. The reaction solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. Then, the target molecules (phenobarbital) were washed with a mixture solvent of acetonitrile/ethanol (v/v,2:8) until no phenobarbital could be detected in the washing solvent by UV–Vis spectrometry. Eventually, MIPs-GSCDs were dried at room temperature. The non-imprinted polymers (NIPs) caped with GSCDs (NIPs-GSCDs) were prepared under the same conditions without addition of the phenobarbital.

1.5 mg of the MIPs-GSCDs composite was dispersed into the 3 mL universal buffer (pH = 8.0) and the recognition capacity of MIPs-GSCDs was measured using fluorescence spectroscopy in different phenobarbital concentrations and the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The fluorescence intensity at 410 nm (the maximum emission wavelength) was measured in the presence and absence of phenobarbital and recorded as F and F₀, respectively. The values of F₀-F were used to determine phenobarbital concentration. The controlled experiments were done using NIPs-GSCDs similar to MIPs-GSCDs.

All blood plasma samples were collected from the Isfahan University of Medical Sciences’ Health Center. To remove the proteins, 5 mL of plasma sample and 5 mL of 10% (w/v) trichloroacetic acid were mixed in a centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.45-µm Millipore filter to eliminate particulate matters. All mixtures were diluted with universal buffer to one-tenth of their original concentrations. Afterward, 300 µL of the prepared sample solutions and different amounts of phenobarbital standard solution were transferred to a cell including 500 mg L−1 of the MIPs-GSCDs composite at pH = 8.0. Finally, phenobarbital was measured using fluorescence measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GSCDs, MIPs-GSCDs, and NIPs-GSCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM image of GSCDs is shown in Fig. 1A. The average diameter of the GSCDs is about 5.3 nm. The TEM image of MIPs-GSCDs and NIPs-GSCDs were shown in Fig. 1B and C, respectively. The resulting images show that the morphology of NIPs-GSCDs was similar to that of MIPs-GSCDs and the diameters of these composites were about 47 nm.

The DLS data for GSCDs is shown in Fig. 2A and confirmed that the average size of GSCDs is 5.5 nm. The zeta potential of GSCDs was calculated as −10.5 mV. The negative charges are preventing the accumulation of particles and causing the stability of GSCDs. Fig. 2B shows the XRD pattern of GSCDs which exhibited a broad peak at 2Ө = 24.7° corresponding to the (002) diffraction planes of carbon materials and is also indicative of the amorphous structure in the GSCDs [18].

Fig. 2C shows the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the GSCDs. Fig. 2D shows the fluorescence spectra of the GSCDs observed at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 to 440 nm. The maximum fluorescence emission intensity was observed at 410 nm when excited at 340 nm.

The FT-IR spectra of GSCDs are shown in Fig. 2E-a. The peak at 3380 cm−1 corresponds to -OH and -NH stretching vibrations and -CH groups’ stretching vibrations are seen at 2906 cm−1. Similarly, the peak at 1636 cm−1 was associated with C˭C stretching vibrations. Simultaneously, C-N bending vibrations were observed at 1332 cm−1 and the peak at 1071 cm−1 is associated with C-O bending vibrations. FT-IR spectra of NIPs-GSCDs, MIPs-GSCDs after elution, MIPs-GSCDs before elution and phenobarbital are shown in Fig. 2E-b-e, respectively. For FT-IR spectra of NIPs-GSCDs and MIPs-GSCDs, the strong and broad absorption peaks at about 1050 cm−1 correspond to Si-O-Si asymmetric stretching and the peak at about 800 cm−1 is associated with the Si-O vibration. The peaks at 470 cm−1 correspond to O-Si-O vibration. All these bands confirmed that the MIPs have been successfully formed on the surface of GSCDs. In Fig. 2E-d the -NH stretch vibration of a secondary amine and -NH-CO-NH- stretch vibration of the barbital ring of phenobarbital were observed at 3000–3400 cm−1 and 1600–1800 cm−1, respectively. After elution with extracting solvents, this band vanished in the FT-IR spectrum shown in Fig. 2E-c. This shows that the phenobarbital was successfully imprinted.

There are two methods used to prepare silica-coated CDs, the Stӧbar and reverse micro emulsion methods [19,20]. The Stӧbar method is more complicated compared to the reverse micro emulsion method and the particles size distribution cannot be controlled with the accuracy of the reverse micro emulsion method [21].

In this work, the reverse micro emulsion method was used. Here, cyclohexane and Triton X-100 were used as a continuous phase and as a surfactant for stabilizing of the microscale water droplets covering fluorescence GSCDs, respectively. The formation of silica began by hydrolyzing TEOS at the cyclohexane/water interface, catalyzed by ammonium hydroxide. Then, in situ growth of silica layer continued by adsorbing the hydrolyzed TEOS [22]. After formation of thin silica film, MIPs were obtained with the copolymerization of APTES and TEOS on the surface of silica-coated GSCDs [23]. The structures of phenobarbital, TEOS and APTES are shown in Fig. 3. According to Fig. 3, target molecules interacted with APTES through the hydrogen bond interactions and van der Waals forces. MIPs-GSCDs can rebind the targets via the non-covalent interactions after removal of target molecules. This causes the fluorescence intensity of MIPs-GSCDs to be quenched due to rebinding of phenobarbital. Fig. 4A shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of extracted mixtures. As can be seen, phenobarbital was not detected by UV–Vis spectrophotometer after 4 washing cycles. Commonly, the fluorescence of MIPs can be quenched via two different mechanisms; energy transfer and/or charge transfer. The energy transfer can be verified by means of comparing the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the target molecule and fluorescence spectrum of the fluorophore (MIPs) [16,18]. If the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the target molecule overlaps with the fluorescence spectrum of the fluorophore, the target molecule can absorb the fluorescence emission of the MIPs and as a result, energy transfer occurs [16,18]. In the present study, as can be seen in the Fig. 4B that the UV–Vis spectrum of the target molecule (phenobarbital) and fluorescence spectrum of MIPs-GSCDs do not overlap. Therefore, the energy transfer mechanism is eliminated as a possibility and the other mechanism, i.e., the charge transfer, is more probable.

Fig. 5A shows the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity. The optimum pH was 8 and was selected for further experiments. At pH <7 the response of the sensor was reduced because the pkₐ of phenobarbital is 7.63 [2] which caused the weakening of hydrogen bonding between the phenobarbital and APTES. The response decreased at high pH is due to the occurrence of surface defects [24].

To determine the optimum amount of MIPs-GSCDs composites, fluorescence intensities were recorded at different ratios of fresh MIPs-GSCDs (160–650 mg L−1) to 4.0 nmol L−1 of phenobarbital at pH = 8.0. Fig. 5B shows that the optimum amount of MIPs-GSCDs was 500 mg L−1. Spectrofluorimeter is unable to record higher amounts of MIPs-GSCDs and sensitivity is reduced at lower amounts of MIPs-GSCDs due to a decrease in signal to noise ratio.

The fluorescence intensity was recorded at different temperature (25–50 °C) for 4.0 nmol L−1 of phenobarbital at pH = 8.0. The optimum temperature is shown in Fig. 5C. The fluorescence intensity of the MIPs-GSCDs decreased with increase in the temperature. As a result, room temperature was selected as an optimum temperature for the following experiments.

The effect of time was studied for MIPs-GSCDs fluorescence intensity. To determine the optimum time, a certain amount of phenobarbital (4.0 nmol L−1) was mixed with MIPs-GSCDs (500 mg L−1) at pH 8.0 and the fluorescence intensities were recorded from 0 to 20 min at room temperature. The results are shown in Fig. 5D. The response time increased with time up to 10 min and then remained almost unchanged. Therefore, 10 min was selected as an optimum time.

The fluorescence emission spectra of the MIPs-GSCDs and NIPs-GSCDs in the presence of different concentrations of phenobarbital were used to compare the recognition ability of the MIPs-GSCDs versus that of NIPs-GSCDs. The results are shown in Fig. 6A and B. The fluorescence quenching can be described by the Stern-Volmer equation, F₀/F = 1 + Ksᵥ (Q) where Ksᵥ is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and Q is the concentration of the phenobarbital. Fig. 6C and D show the relationship between F₀/F vs. phenobarbital concentration and F0-F vs. phenobarbital concentration, respectively. According to these Figs., F₀-F shows a wider linear dynamic range than F₀/F. Therefore, F0-F vs. phenobarbital concentration was used as the calibration curve.

In the next step, the calibration curve of MIPs-GSCDs was created and the regression equation F₀-F = 16.796 CPhenobarbital + 82.394 was calculated. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.1 nmol L−1 (according to 3 Sb/m, where m is the slope of the calibration curve and Sb is the blank standard deviation (n = 6)) and the linear range was 0.4–34.5 nmol L−1. The relative standard deviations (RSD) of 7.3% was observed for 1.2 nmol L−1 phenobarbital (n = 3). The offered sensor was highly sensitive and selective for phenobarbital detection. The analytical Figs. of merit of this method and other methods used for phenobarbital detection are shown in Table 1. The results show that the method has a low detection limit and an appropriate dynamic range compared to other methods.

Selectivity is one of the most important analytical parameters. The selectivity of this method was evaluated by testing the response of the MIPs-GSCDs to other materials such as dopamine, ascorbic acid, tryptophan, glucose, cysteine, primidone, K⁺, Mg2⁺, uric acid, glutathione, adrenaline, histamine, ibuprofen, epinephrine and tryptamine (all at the 300.0 nmol L−1) in the presence of phenobarbital at 4.0 nmol L−1. The results suggested that MIPs-GSCDs were specific to phenobarbital but nonspecific to other materials because the phenobarbital interacts with imprinted cavities in MIPs-GSCDs while MIPs-GSCDs nonspecific binds with other materials. As shown in Fig. 7, the selectivity of MIPs-GSCDs, NIPs-GSCDs, and GSCDs was investigated. The results show that NIPs-GSCDs and GSCDs were nonspecific to phenobarbital while MIPs-GSCDs were specific to phenobarbital.

Biological studies were carried out in human blood plasma samples to appraise the ability of the MIPs-GSCDs for phenobarbital determination. For this purpose, the samples were prepared as described in the real sample preparation section and analyzed using the standard addition method. The results indicated the accuracy of the proposed method for determination of phenobarbital. ( Table 2)

In this work, we demonstrated the construction of a fluorescence sensor is based on MIPs-GSCDs for determination of phenobarbital. In the first step, GSCDs were synthesized from Cedrus by hydrothermal method. In the next step, MIPs-GSCDs were synthesized using imprinting reverse micro emulsion technique. The results showed that MIPs-GSCDs were specific to phenobarbital but nonspecific to other materials. This new sensor has advantages of being simple, low cost, rapid, independent of organic solvents, easy to use and a low detection limit. The sensor was used for determination of phenobarbital in real samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support of Isfahan University of Technology (IUT)’s Research Council and Center of Excellence in Sensor and Green Chemistry.
